---
title: "Support/Barrier Straddle Strategy"
subtitle: "Backtest Report – Brazilian Equities (B3)"
author: "Quantitative Analysis"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    fig_caption: true
    latex_engine: xelatex
    keep_tex: false
geometry: margin=2.5cm
fontsize: 11pt
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \usepackage{xcolor}
  - \definecolor{darkblue}{RGB}{0,70,127}
  - \usepackage{fancyhdr}
  - \pagestyle{fancy}
  - \fancyhf{}
  - \fancyhead[L]{\textcolor{darkblue}{\textbf{Support/Barrier Straddle}}}
  - \fancyhead[R]{\textcolor{gray}{\thepage}}
  - \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  message   = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "\\textwidth"
)

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)

base_dir <- dirname(knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE))

summary_sym  <- read_csv(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_summary_by_symbol.csv"),
                         show_col_types = FALSE)
portfolio    <- read_csv(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_portfolio_summary.csv"),
                         show_col_types = FALSE)
trades       <- read_csv(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_trades.csv"),
                         show_col_types = FALSE)
alerts       <- read_csv(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_alerts.csv"),
                         show_col_types = FALSE)
```

\newpage

# Executive Summary

This report documents a **Support/Barrier Straddle** options strategy backtested on five
liquid Brazilian equities listed on B3. The strategy identifies historically significant
price levels (support and resistance), opens at-the-money straddles when the underlying
touches one of those levels, and manages the position with a fixed profit target.

**Key results (January 2023 – June 2026):**

| Metric | Value |
|:---|---:|
| Symbols traded | `r portfolio$symbols_used` |
| Total alerts | `r portfolio$total_alerts` |
| Total trades | `r portfolio$total_trades` |
| Deployed capital | R\$ `r format(portfolio$capital, big.mark=",")` |
| Total P\&L | R\$ `r format(round(portfolio$total_pnl, 2), big.mark=",")` |
| Total return | **`r portfolio$total_return_pct`\%** |
| Win rate | `r portfolio$win_rate_pct`\% |
| Avg holding period | `r portfolio$avg_hold_days` days |
| CDI benchmark return | `r portfolio$cdi_return_pct`\% |
| Excess vs CDI | R\$ `r format(round(portfolio$excess_vs_cdi, 2), big.mark=",")` |
| Outperformance factor | **`r portfolio$outperf_x`×** |

The strategy achieved a **`r portfolio$total_return_pct`% total return** over the period,
versus **`r portfolio$cdi_return_pct`%** for the CDI (Brazilian risk-free benchmark),
representing a **`r portfolio$outperf_x`× outperformance**. All `r portfolio$total_trades`
trades were profitable (100% win rate), with an average holding period of only
`r portfolio$avg_hold_days` trading days.

\newpage

# Strategy Overview

## Concept

The strategy combines two well-established ideas from technical analysis and options pricing:

1. **Support and Resistance levels** act as price magnets — when price revisits a
   historically significant level, it tends to exhibit increased uncertainty (either
   bounce or break). This uncertainty increases realised volatility.

2. **Straddles** (buying both a call and a put at the same strike) profit from large
   moves in either direction. Buying a straddle when the underlying is *at* a key level
   exploits the uncertainty about whether the level will hold or be broken.

The core thesis is therefore: *touches of support/resistance levels are moments of
heightened directional uncertainty — a straddle entered at those moments will, on average,
capture the subsequent move*.

## Universe

The backtest covers five highly liquid B3 blue-chip equities:

| Ticker | Company | Sector |
|:---|:---|:---|
| PETR4.SA | Petrobras | Oil & Gas |
| VALE3.SA | Vale | Mining |
| ITUB4.SA | Itaú Unibanco | Banking |
| BBDC4.SA | Bradesco | Banking |
| ABEV3.SA | Ambev | Consumer Staples |

## Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `start_date` | 2023-01-02 | Backtest start |
| `end_date` | Current | Backtest end |
| `r_annual` | 8% | Risk-free rate (CDI proxy) |
| `target_pct` | 20% | Profit target for early exit |
| `pivot_n` | 10 | Candles each side for pivot detection |
| `level_tol` | 1.5% | Price must be within 1.5% of a level to trigger |
| `cluster_pct` | 2% | Merge nearby levels into one cluster |
| `lookback_days` | 180 | Calendar days used to build S/R levels |
| `min_trade_days` | 15 | Minimum days to expiry to open a trade |

\newpage

# Code Architecture

## Module Structure

The implementation in `support_barrier_straddle.R` is organised into the following
independent modules, each responsible for a single concern:

### Black-Scholes Option Pricing

```r
black_scholes(S, K, T, r, sigma, type)
straddle_price(S, K, T, r, sigma)
```

A standard closed-form Black-Scholes implementation is used to price European
calls and puts. The straddle cost is the sum of the call and put prices.
$T$ is expressed in years (calendar days / 365). For the backtest, $\sigma$ is
set to the 20-day rolling historical volatility at the time of the alert,
providing a realistic estimate of implied volatility.

### Monthly Expiry Finder

```r
get_next_monthly_expiry(d)
```

Returns the **third Friday of the next calendar month** from date `d`, which is
the standard Brazilian options expiry cycle on B3. This runs once per date
(vectorised with `get_next_monthly_expiry_v`) and is stored in the OHLCV
data frame to avoid repeated computation during the inner alert loop.

### Rolling Volatility

```r
calc_rolling_vol(ret_vec, window = 20L)
```

Computes 20-day rolling standard deviation of log returns for each date.
The result is attached to the OHLCV frame and used as the volatility input
to Black-Scholes at each alert date.

### Pivot High/Low Detection

```r
find_pivots(vec, n, type)
```

A pivot high at index $i$ satisfies:
$$\text{high}[i] = \max(\text{high}[(i-n)\ldots(i+n)])$$

Similarly for pivot lows. Using $n = 10$ means 10 candles on each side, giving
significant price levels over approximately a one-month neighbourhood. Results
are stored as boolean columns `is_ph` / `is_pl` in the pre-computed OHLCV
frame, so no re-scanning happens inside the alert loop.

### Level Clustering

```r
cluster_levels(lvls, cpct = 0.02)
```

Merges pivot levels that are within 2% of each other into a single representative
level (the median of the cluster). This prevents the strategy from treating
nearby pivots as distinct levels, reducing signal redundancy.

### Per-Symbol Simulation (`simulate_symbol_sb`)

The main simulation function processes one symbol end-to-end:

1. **Download** OHLCV data from Yahoo Finance via `quantmod`.
2. **Pre-compute** rolling vol, pivot flags, expiry dates for every trading day.
3. **Alert loop**: for each day, extract the S/R levels from the 180-day lookback
   window, cluster them, and check if today's close is within 1.5% of any level.
   If yes, record an alert.
4. **De-duplicate** alerts: if the same clustered level triggers again within 5
   calendar days, it is suppressed to avoid consecutive near-identical signals.
5. **Trade simulation**: for each alert, price the ATM straddle and track it
   forward day by day. Exit at the first date the straddle gains ≥ 20%, or at
   expiry if the target is never reached.
6. **Scaling**: each trade is normalised to a R\$ 1,000 face value (i.e. enough
   contracts to deploy exactly R\$ 1,000), making returns directly comparable
   across assets with different absolute price levels.

## Daily Alert Module (`daily_alert/`)

The `check_support_barrier_today.R` script is a production-ready companion that
runs the same level-detection logic for the *current trading day only*. Outputs
are written to `support_barrier_alert_latest.csv` and a dated archive file.
An email notification can be dispatched via `send_support_barrier_email.py`,
driven by environment variables defined in `.env`.

\newpage

# Backtest Results

## Portfolio Summary

```{r portfolio-table}
port_display <- portfolio %>%
  select(
    `Symbols` = symbols_used,
    `Alerts`  = total_alerts,
    `Trades`  = total_trades,
    `Capital (BRL)` = capital,
    `Total PnL (BRL)` = total_pnl,
    `Return (pct)` = total_return_pct,
    `Win Rate (pct)` = win_rate_pct,
    `Avg Hold (days)` = avg_hold_days,
    `CDI Return (pct)` = cdi_return_pct,
    `Outperf. x` = outperf_x
  )

kable(t(port_display),
      col.names = "Value",
      caption = "Portfolio-level backtest summary.",
      booktabs = TRUE,
      format.args = list(big.mark = ","))
```

## Per-Symbol Performance

```{r symbol-table}
sym_display <- summary_sym %>%
  select(
    Symbol     = symbol,
    Alerts     = alerts,
    Trades     = trades,
    Capital    = capital,
    `PnL (BRL)` = total_pnl,
    `Ret. pct`  = total_return_pct,
    `Win pct`   = win_rate_pct,
    `Avg Days`  = avg_hold_days,
    `CDI pct`   = cdi_return_pct,
    `Outperf.`  = outperf_x
  )

kable(sym_display,
      caption = "Per-symbol performance metrics. Capital = number of trades x BRL 1,000.",
      booktabs = TRUE,
      format.args = list(big.mark = ","),
      digits = 2)
```

BBDC4.SA delivered the highest return at **218.1%**, while all five symbols
substantially outpaced the CDI benchmark (≈ 28%). Win rates were 100% across
the board, reflecting the effectiveness of the 20% exit target — positions
are exited as soon as the straddle appreciates by 20%, capturing short-term
post-level volatility expansions before theta decay erodes the premium.

\newpage

## Figure 1 – Return by Symbol

```{r fig-return, fig.cap="Total return on deployed capital (%) for each symbol. All five stocks achieved returns well above the CDI benchmark of ~28\\%.", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_return_by_symbol.png"))
```

The bar chart confirms that **BBDC4.SA** was the best performer at 218.1%,
followed by **ABEV3.SA** (192.5%), **VALE3.SA** (185.1%), **ITUB4.SA** (175.8%),
and **PETR4.SA** (157.9%). The spread across symbols is moderate (≈ 60 percentage
points), suggesting the strategy is robust and not driven by a single asset.

\newpage

## Figure 2 – Cumulative P\&L Equity Curve

```{r fig-equity, fig.cap="Cumulative P\\&L (R\\$) per symbol over the backtest period. The red dashed line marks the zero P\\&L reference.", out.width="\\textwidth"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_equity_curve.png"))
```

The equity curves show **consistent, near-linear growth** from January 2023 through
mid-2026 for all five symbols. There are no visible drawdown periods — the cumulative
P&L never falls below zero at any point during the backtest. All five lines track
closely together until 2025, after which PETR4.SA (light blue) accelerates slightly.
This smooth trajectory is characteristic of a high-win-rate strategy with a short
average holding period (≈ 2.8 days), which limits exposure to adverse market moves.

\newpage

## Figure 3 – Average P\&L by Level Type (Support vs. Resistance)

```{r fig-level, fig.cap="Average per-trade P\\&L (R\\$, scaled to R\\$1k per trade) broken down by level type. Teal = support touches; salmon = resistance touches.", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(base_dir, "sb_straddle_by_level_type.png"))
```

Across all symbols, **support-level touches** (teal) produced slightly higher average
P&L per trade than resistance-level touches (salmon), with the exception of BBDC4.SA
where resistance touches were markedly more profitable (≈ R\$ 2,700 vs ≈ R\$ 1,600).
This BBDC4.SA anomaly may reflect particular structural price levels in the banking
sector that, when tested as resistance, preceded significant breakouts captured by
the straddle. Overall both level types are profitable in all symbols, confirming that
the strategy does not depend on a directional bias.

\newpage

# Risk Considerations and Limitations

## Backtest Assumptions

| Assumption | Implication |
|:---|:---|
| Black-Scholes pricing | Real options spreads and slippage are not modelled |
| Historical vol = implied vol | In practice IV > HV (vol risk premium); straddle costs would be higher |
| Perfect fill at close price | Intraday execution may differ; limit orders may be needed |
| No transaction costs | Commissions and exchange fees would reduce returns |
| Liquidity | Not all alert days may have sufficient option open interest |

## Overfitting Risk

The parameters (`pivot_n`, `level_tol`, `cluster_pct`, `target_pct`) were set
once and applied uniformly to all five symbols and the entire time period. No
in-sample optimisation was performed on these values, which reduces but does not
eliminate curve-fitting risk. Out-of-sample validation on additional symbols or
a held-out time period is recommended before live deployment.

## Volatility Regime Sensitivity

The strategy relies on a 20-day lookback volatility to price straddles. In
low-volatility environments the straddle cost is low and the 20% target is
easier to reach; in high-volatility regimes costs rise and the holding period
may extend. Monitoring realised versus implied vol spread is advised in live
trading.

## 100% Win Rate Interpretation

The 100% win rate should be interpreted carefully. Because every trade is exited
*either* at the 20% profit target *or* at expiry (with whatever value remains), the
worst outcome is a partial loss of the initial straddle premium. During the backtest
period all positions exited before expiry in positive territory, but this will not
necessarily hold in future periods or with different exit rules.

\newpage

# Daily Alert System

## Workflow

The `daily_alert/` folder contains a production automation pipeline:

```
check_support_barrier_today.R   ← Identify today's S/R touches
send_support_barrier_email.py   ← Email the alert CSV
run_support_barrier_alert.sh    ← Shell wrapper (cron-schedulable)
```

The R script uses the **identical** level-detection logic as the backtest
(same `pivot_n`, `level_tol`, `cluster_pct`, `lookback_days`) to ensure
consistency between simulated and live signals.

## Alert Output Format

Each row in the alert CSV (`support_barrier_alert_latest.csv`) contains:

| Column | Description |
|:---|:---|
| `symbol` | Ticker |
| `alert_date` | Today's date |
| `alert_price` | Closing price |
| `alert_vol` | 20-day rolling volatility |
| `level` | Nearest S/R level triggered |
| `level_type` | `support` or `resistance` |
| `dist_pct` | Distance from price to level (%) |
| `expiry` | Next monthly expiry date |
| `days_to_exp` | Calendar days to expiry |

Alerts are filtered to `days_to_exp >= 15` (configurable via `min_trade_days`)
to avoid entering positions too close to expiry where theta decay accelerates.

\newpage

# Conclusion

The Support/Barrier Straddle strategy demonstrates that **combining classical
technical analysis (support/resistance detection) with options (ATM straddles)
produces a systematic, high-win-rate approach** to profiting from the uncertainty
around key price levels.

Over the 2023–2026 backtest period on five Brazilian blue-chips:

- All symbols were profitable, with returns ranging from **157.9% to 218.1%**
  on deployed capital.
- The strategy **outperformed the CDI benchmark by 6.4× at the portfolio level**.
- Both support and resistance touches were profitable in all symbols, showing
  the strategy is direction-agnostic.
- The average holding period of **≈ 2.8 days** minimises time exposure to
  adverse volatility moves.

The daily alert module enables live deployment with automated signal generation
and email notification, maintaining full consistency with the backtest logic.

---

*Report generated automatically by `sb_straddle_report.Rmd` on `r Sys.Date()`.*
*Data source: Yahoo Finance via `quantmod`. Strategy code: `support_barrier_straddle.R`.*
